blocks.text: Agents are excellent at emitting **structured data** and unreliable at writing bespoke frontend code. A2UI resolves that mismatch: the agent emits a declarative JSON payload; a renderer maps every block to a vetted, pre-built component. The UI is *data*, not code.

items.label: Type
items.text: Every block is keyed by `type` (never `component`) plus typed fields — e.g. {"type": "stat_card", "value": "99.98%", "label": "Uptime"}

items.label: Dispatch
items.text: The renderer looks up the type in its registry and calls that one renderer function with the block

items.label: Fail visible
items.text: Unknown fields are ignored; unknown types render a visible error — never a silent drop

tabs.label: What renders
tabs.code:
A stat card with an upward delta and a
progress bar — identical on every surface
that carries those atoms. No HTML written.

blocks.text: **Why declarative beats generated code:** a payload is reviewable at a glance, validatable against a schema before it ever renders, carries no script-injection surface, ports across surfaces unchanged, and caches like data because it *is* data. Generated HTML/JS has none of those properties.

points: A2UI = agent emits typed JSON blocks; renderer supplies all presentation
Blocks are keyed by `type`; fields are the atom's typed contract
Declarative payloads are reviewable, validatable, portable, and injection-free
The renderer's registry is the security boundary: only vetted components ever run

blocks.question: What does a block's `type` field do?
options: Names a CSS class applied to the block
Selects which vetted renderer function receives the block
Declares the JavaScript framework to load
blocks.explanation: Dispatch is the whole model: `type` looks up one pre-built, audited renderer function in the registry. The agent never ships behavior — only data.
